Force at G is {tex}F_1 - (F_2 sin 30° + F_3 sin 30°){/tex} {tex}F = 2Gm^2 -{/tex} {tex}\left(2 G m^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}+2 G m^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} = 0 When mass at A is 2m, then gravitational force on mass 2m at G due to mass 2m at A is Net force, F 1 is G {tex}\frac{2 m \times 2 m}{1^{2}}{/tex} = 4Gm 2 along GA Net force getting at G due to masses A, B and C is given by: {tex}= F_1 - (F_2 sin 30^o + F_3 sin 30^o){/tex} = {tex}4 Gm^2 -{/tex} {tex}\left(2 G m^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}+2 G m^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}= 2 Gm^2{/tex} along GA.

question_text: Find the potential energy of a system of four particles placed at the vertices of a square of side l. Also, obtain the potential at the centre of the square.
question_html: Find the potential energy of a system of four particles placed at the vertices of a square of side l. Also, obtain the potential at the centre of the square.
solution_text: Consider four masses each of mass m at the corners of a square of side l; We have four mass pairs at distance l and two diagonal pairs at distance {tex}\sqrt{2} l{/tex} Hence, {tex}\sqrt{2} \iota{/tex} W(r) {tex}=-4 \frac{\mathrm{G} \mathrm{m}^{2}}{l}-2 \frac{G \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\sqrt{2} l}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{2 G m^{2}}{l}\left(2+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=-5.41 \frac{G m^{2}}{l}{/tex} The gravitational potential at the centre of the square {tex}(r=\sqrt{2} l / 2){/tex} is {tex}U(r)=-4 \sqrt{2} \frac{\mathrm{Gm}}{l}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider four masses each of mass m at the corners of a square of side l;

We have four mass pairs at distance l and two diagonal pairs at distance {tex}\sqrt{2} l{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\sqrt{2} \iota{/tex}
W(r) {tex}=-4 \frac{\mathrm{G} \mathrm{m}^{2}}{l}-2 \frac{G \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\sqrt{2} l}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{2 G m^{2}}{l}\left(2+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=-5.41 \frac{G m^{2}}{l}{/tex}
The gravitational potential at the centre of the square {tex}(r=\sqrt{2} l / 2){/tex} is
{tex}U(r)=-4 \sqrt{2} \frac{\mathrm{Gm}}{l}{/tex}

question_text: Two uniform solid spheres of equal radii R, but mass M and 4M have a centre to centre separation 6R, as shown in Fig. The two spheres are held fixed. A projectile of mass m is projected from the surface of the sphere of mass M directly towards the centre of the second sphere. Obtain an expression for the minimum speed v of the projectile so that it reaches the surface of the second sphere.
question_html: Two uniform solid spheres of equal radii R, but mass M and 4M have a centre to centre separation 6R, as shown in Fig. The two spheres are held fixed. A projectile of mass m is projected from the surface of the sphere of mass M directly towards the centre of the second sphere. Obtain an expression for the minimum speed v of the projectile so that it reaches the surface of the second sphere.
solution_text: The projectile is acted upon by two mutually opposing gravitational forces of the two spheres. The neutral point N (see Fig.) is defined as the position where the two forces cancel each other exactly. If ON = r, we have {tex}\frac{G M m}{r^{2}}=\frac{4 G M m}{(6 R-r)^{2}}{/tex} (6R - r) 2 = 4r 2 6R - r = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2r r = 2R or - 6R The neutral point r = – 6R does not concern us in this example. Thus ON = r = 2R. It is sufficient to project the particle with a speed that would enable it to reach N. Thereafter, the greater gravitational pull of 4M would suffice. The mechanical energy at the surface of M is {tex}E_{l}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\frac{G M m}{R}-\frac{4 G M m}{5 R}{/tex} At the neutral point N, the speed approaches zero. The mechanical energy at N is purely potential. {tex}E_{N}=-\frac{G M m}{2 R}-\frac{4 G M m}{4 R}{/tex} From the principle of conservation of mechanical energy {tex}\frac{1}{2} v^{2}-\frac{G M}{R}-\frac{4 G M}{5 R}=-\frac{G M}{2 R}-\frac{G M}{R}{/tex} or {tex}v^{2}=\frac{2 G M}{R}\left(\frac{4}{5}-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}v=\left(\frac{3 G M}{5 R}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex} A point to note is that the speed of the projectile is zero at N, but is nonzero when it strikes the heavier sphere 4M.
solution_html:
The projectile is acted upon by two mutually opposing gravitational forces of the two spheres. The neutral point N (see Fig.) is defined as the position where the two forces cancel each other exactly. If ON = r, we have
{tex}\frac{G M m}{r^{2}}=\frac{4 G M m}{(6 R-r)^{2}}{/tex}
(6R - r)2 = 4r2
6R - r = {tex}\pm{/tex}2r
r = 2R or - 6R
The neutral point r = – 6R does not concern us in this example. Thus ON = r = 2R. It is sufficient to project the particle with a speed that would enable it to reach N. Thereafter, the greater gravitational pull of 4M would suffice. The mechanical energy at the surface of M is
{tex}E_{l}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\frac{G M m}{R}-\frac{4 G M m}{5 R}{/tex}
At the neutral point N, the speed approaches zero. The mechanical energy at N is purely potential.
{tex}E_{N}=-\frac{G M m}{2 R}-\frac{4 G M m}{4 R}{/tex}
From the principle of conservation of mechanical energy
{tex}\frac{1}{2} v^{2}-\frac{G M}{R}-\frac{4 G M}{5 R}=-\frac{G M}{2 R}-\frac{G M}{R}{/tex}
or 
{tex}v^{2}=\frac{2 G M}{R}\left(\frac{4}{5}-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}v=\left(\frac{3 G M}{5 R}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}
A point to note is that the speed of the projectile is zero at N, but is nonzero when it strikes the heavier sphere 4M.

question_text: The planet Mars has two moons, Phobos and Deimos. Phobos has a period 7h, 39 min and an orbital radius of 9.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 km. Calculate the mass of mars. Assume that earth and mars move in circular orbits around the sun with the Martian orbit being 1.52 times the orbital radius of the earth. What is the length of the Martian year in days?
question_html:
The planet Mars has two moons, Phobos and Deimos.

  1. Phobos has a period 7h, 39 min and an orbital radius of 9.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 km. Calculate the mass of mars.
  2. Assume that earth and mars move in circular orbits around the sun with the Martian orbit being 1.52 times the orbital radius of the earth. What is the length of the Martian year in days?
solution_text: {tex}T = 7 h\ 39 min = 459\times 60 s{/tex} , orbital radius is given by R and {tex}R = 9.4 \times 10^3 km = 9.4 \times10^6 m{/tex} , {tex}M_m ={/tex} ? {tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of mars is given by, {tex}M_m ={/tex} {tex}\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{G} \cdot \frac{R^{3}}{T^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \times(3.14)^{2} \times\left(9.4 \times 10^{6}\right)^{3}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times(459 \times 60)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex} 6.48\times 10^{23} kg{/tex} Using Kepler's third law, {tex}\frac{T_{m}^{2}}{T^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R_{M S}^{3}}{R_{E S}^{3}}{/tex} where R MS is the mass-sun distance R ES is the earth-sun distance {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}T_m = (1.52)^{3/2} \times 365 = 684\ days{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}T = 7 h\ 39 min = 459\times  60 s{/tex} , orbital radius is given by R and 
    {tex}R = 9.4 \times 10^3 km = 9.4 \times10^6 m{/tex}, {tex}M_m ={/tex} ?
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of mars is given by, {tex}M_m ={/tex} {tex}\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{G} \cdot \frac{R^{3}}{T^{2}}{/tex}
    =     {tex}\frac{4 \times(3.14)^{2} \times\left(9.4 \times 10^{6}\right)^{3}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times(459 \times 60)^{2}}{/tex}
    = {tex} 6.48\times  10^{23} kg{/tex}
  2. Using Kepler's third law,  {tex}\frac{T_{m}^{2}}{T^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R_{M S}^{3}}{R_{E S}^{3}}{/tex}
    where RMS is the mass-sun distance RES is the earth-sun distance
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}T_m = (1.52)^{3/2} \times 365 = 684\ days{/tex}

question_text: Weighing the Earth: You are given the following data: g = 9.81 ms -2 , R E = 6.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m, the distance to the moon R = 3.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m, and the time period of the moon’s revolution is 27.3 days. Obtain the mass of the Earth M E in two different ways.
question_html: Weighing the Earth: You are given the following data: g = 9.81 ms-2, RE = 6.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m, the distance to the moon R = 3.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m, and the time period of the moon’s revolution is 27.3 days. Obtain the mass of the Earth ME in two different ways.
solution_text: {tex}g=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{G M_{E}}{R_{E}^{2}}{/tex} , we have {tex}M_{E}=\frac{g R_{E}^{2}}{G}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9.81 \times\left(6.37 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11}}{/tex} {tex}=5.97 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{kg}{/tex} The moon is a satellite of the Earth. From the derivation of Kepler’s third law {tex}T^{2}=\frac{4 \pi^{2} R^{3}}{G M_{E}}{/tex} {tex}M_{E}=\frac{4 \pi^{2} R^{3}}{G T^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \times 3.14 \times 3.14 \times(3.84)^{3} \times 10^{24}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times(27.3 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60)^{2}}{/tex} = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 kg Both methods yield almost the same answer, the difference between them being less than 1%.
solution_html:
{tex}g=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{G M_{E}}{R_{E}^{2}}{/tex}, we have
{tex}M_{E}=\frac{g R_{E}^{2}}{G}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9.81 \times\left(6.37 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11}}{/tex}
{tex}=5.97 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{kg}{/tex}
The moon is a satellite of the Earth. From the derivation of Kepler’s third law
{tex}T^{2}=\frac{4 \pi^{2} R^{3}}{G M_{E}}{/tex}
{tex}M_{E}=\frac{4 \pi^{2} R^{3}}{G T^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4 \times 3.14 \times 3.14 \times(3.84)^{3} \times 10^{24}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times(27.3 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60)^{2}}{/tex}
= 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 kg
Both methods yield almost the same answer, the difference between them being less than 1%.

question_text: A 400 kg satellite is in a circular orbit of radius 2R E about the Earth. How much energy is required to transfer it to a circular orbit of radius 4R E ? What are the changes in the kinetic and potential energies?
question_html: A 400 kg satellite is in a circular orbit of radius 2RE about the Earth. How much energy is required to transfer it to a circular orbit of radius 4RE? What are the changes in the kinetic and potential energies?
solution_text: Initially, {tex}E_{i}=-\frac{G M_{E} m}{4 R_{E}}{/tex} While finally {tex}E_{f}=-\frac{G M_{E} m}{8 R_{E}}{/tex} The change in the total energy is {tex}\Delta E=E_{f}-E_{i}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{G M_{E} m}{8 R_{E}}=\left(\frac{G M_{E}}{R_{E}^{2}}\right) \frac{m R_{E}}{8}{/tex} {tex}\Delta E=\frac{g m R_{E}}{8}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{9.81 \times 400 \times 6.37 \times {{10}^6}}}{8} = 3.13 \times {10^9}J{/tex} The kinetic energy is reduced and it mimics {tex}\Delta E{/tex} , namely, {tex}\Delta K{/tex} = K f - K i = -3.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 J. The change in potential energy is twice the change in the total energy, namely {tex}\Delta V=V_{f}-V_{i}=-6.25 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
solution_html:
Initially,
{tex}E_{i}=-\frac{G M_{E} m}{4 R_{E}}{/tex}
While finally
{tex}E_{f}=-\frac{G M_{E} m}{8 R_{E}}{/tex}
The change in the total energy is
{tex}\Delta E=E_{f}-E_{i}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{G M_{E} m}{8 R_{E}}=\left(\frac{G M_{E}}{R_{E}^{2}}\right) \frac{m R_{E}}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta E=\frac{g m R_{E}}{8}{/tex}{tex} = \frac{{9.81 \times 400 \times 6.37 \times {{10}^6}}}{8} = 3.13 \times {10^9}J{/tex} 
The kinetic energy is reduced and it mimics {tex}\Delta E{/tex}, namely, {tex}\Delta K{/tex} = Kf - Ki = -3.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 J.
The change in potential energy is twice the change in the total energy, namely
{tex}\Delta V=V_{f}-V_{i}=-6.25 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{J}{/tex}

question_text: You can shield a charge from electrical forces by putting it inside a hollow conductor. Can you shield a body from the gravitational influence of nearby matter by putting it inside a hollow sphere or by some other means?
question_html: You can shield a charge from electrical forces by putting it inside a hollow conductor. Can you shield a body from the gravitational influence of nearby matter by putting it inside a hollow sphere or by some other means?
solution_text: We cannot shield a body from the gravitational influence of nearby matter, because the gravitational force on a body due to nearby matter is independent of the presence of other matter, whereas it is not so in case of electric forces. It means the gravitational screens are not possible.

It means the gravitational screens are not possible.

question_text: An astronaut inside a small space ship orbiting around the earth cannot detect gravity. If the space station orbiting around the earth has a large size, can he hope to detect gravity?
question_html: An astronaut inside a small space ship orbiting around the earth cannot detect gravity. If the space station orbiting around the earth has a large size, can he hope to detect gravity?
solution_text: Yes, If the spaceship is large enough then the astronaut will definitely detect the Earth’s gravity as Gravitational Force on the spaceship is directly proportional to the mass of spaceship so as a mass of bigger spaceship will be large and hence it will experience a noticeable amount of force which can be detected, we know gravitational force on a body is given as {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_{1} \mathrm{m}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex} Where, F is the gravitational force G is the universal gravitational Constant m 1 is mass of first body which in this case is earth m 2 is the mass of second body which is the spaceship and r is the distance between earth and spaceship so as the mass of spaceship m 2 increases the gravitational force experienced by it increases and hence can be detected i.e. Gravity can be detected.
solution_html:
Yes,
If the spaceship is large enough then the astronaut will definitely detect the Earth’s gravity as Gravitational Force on the spaceship is directly proportional to the mass of spaceship so as a mass of bigger spaceship will be large and hence it will experience a noticeable amount of force which can be detected, we know gravitational force on a body is given as
{tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_{1} \mathrm{m}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}{/tex}
Where, F is the gravitational force
G is the universal gravitational Constant
m1 is mass of first body which in this case is earth
m2 is the mass of second body which is the spaceship
and r is the distance between earth and spaceship
so as the mass of spaceship m2 increases the gravitational force experienced by it increases and hence can be detected i.e. Gravity can be detected.

question_text: If you compare the gravitational force on the earth due to the sun to that due to the moon, you would find that the sun's pull is greater than the moon's pull. However, the tidal effect of the moon's pull is greater than the tidal effect of the sun. Why?
question_html: If you compare the gravitational force on the earth due to the sun to that due to the moon, you would find that the sun's pull is greater than the moon's pull. However, the tidal effect of the moon's pull is greater than the tidal effect of the sun. Why?
solution_text: The tidal effect depends inversely on the cube of the distance unlike force which depends inversely on the square of the distance. Since the distance of moon from the ocean water is very small as compared to the distance of sun from the ocean water on earth. Therefore, the tidal effect of Moon’s pull is greater than the tidal effect of the sun. The ratio of these two effects is {tex}\frac{T_{m}}{T_{s}}=\left(\frac{d_{s}}{d_{m}}\right)^{3}=\left(\frac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{3.8 \times 10^{8}}\right)^{3}{/tex} = 61.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6

question_text: Choose the correct alternatives. Acceleration due to gravity increases/decreases with increasing altitude. Acceleration due to gravity increases/decreases with increasing depth (assume the earth to be a sphere of uniform density). Acceleration due to gravity is independent of the mass of the earth/mass of the body. The formula, - GMm {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r_{2}}-\frac{1}{r_{1}}\right){/tex} is more/less accurate than the formula mg (r 2 - r 1 ) for the difference of potential energy between two points r 2 and r 1 distance away from the centre of the earth.
question_html:
Choose the correct alternatives.

  1. Acceleration due to gravity increases/decreases with increasing altitude.
  2. Acceleration due to gravity increases/decreases with increasing depth (assume the earth to be a sphere of uniform density).
  3. Acceleration due to gravity is independent of the mass of the earth/mass of the body.
  4. The formula, - GMm {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r_{2}}-\frac{1}{r_{1}}\right){/tex} is more/less accurate than the formula mg (r2 - r1) for the difference of potential energy between two points r2 and r1 distance away from the centre of the earth.
solution_text: Acceleration due to gravity at altitude h from the earth’s surface is given by g' = {tex}\frac{g}{\left(1+\frac{h}{R_{e}}\right)^{2}}{/tex} where, R e is the radius of the earth. Therefore, acceleration due to gravity decreases with increasing altitude. Acceleration due to gravity at depth d from the earth’s surface is given by g' = g {tex}\left(1-\frac{d}{R_{e}}\right){/tex} where d is the depth and R is radius of earth. Therefore, acceleration due to gravity decreases with increasing depth. Acceleration due to gravity is given by the formula: g = {tex}GM_e\over R_e^2{/tex} . Hence, acceleration due to gravity is independent of the mass of the body. The formula, - GMm {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r_{2}}-\frac{1}{r_{1}}\right){/tex} is more accurate than the formula mg (r 2 -r 1 ) for the difference of potential energy between two points r 2 and r 1 , distance away from the centre of the earth.
solution_html:
1. Acceleration due to gravity at altitude h from the earth’s surface is given by
    g' = {tex}\frac{g}{\left(1+\frac{h}{R_{e}}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
    where, Re is the radius of the earth.
    Therefore, acceleration due to gravity decreases with increasing altitude.
  2. Acceleration due to gravity at depth d from the earth’s surface is given by
    g' = g {tex}\left(1-\frac{d}{R_{e}}\right){/tex}
    where d is the depth and R is radius of earth. Therefore, acceleration due to gravity decreases with increasing depth.
  3. Acceleration due to gravity is given by the formula: g = {tex}GM_e\over R_e^2{/tex}. Hence, acceleration due to gravity is independent of the mass of the body.
  4. The formula, - GMm {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r_{2}}-\frac{1}{r_{1}}\right){/tex}
    is more accurate than the formula mg (r2 -r1) for the difference of potential energy between two points r2 and r1, distance away from the centre of the earth.

question_text: Suppose there existed a planet that went around the sun twice as fast as the earth. What would be its orbital size as compared to that of the earth?
question_html: Suppose there existed a planet that went around the sun twice as fast as the earth. What would be its orbital size as compared to that of the earth?
solution_text: Suppose, T P and T E denote the time periods of the planet and the earth, respectively. If R P and R E denote the corresponding orbital size {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{T_{P}^{2}}{T_{E}^{2}}=\frac{R_{P}^{3}}{R_{E}^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{R_{P}}{R_{E}}\right)^{3}=\left(\frac{T_{P}}{T_{E}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{E}}=\left(\frac{T_{P}}{T_{B}}\right)^{2 / 3}{/tex} Since {tex}T_P ={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} T_{E}, \frac{T_{P}}{T_{E}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{E}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2 / 3}{/tex} = 0.63
solution_html:
Suppose, TP and TE denote the time periods of the planet and the earth, respectively. If RP and RE denote the corresponding orbital size
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{T_{P}^{2}}{T_{E}^{2}}=\frac{R_{P}^{3}}{R_{E}^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{R_{P}}{R_{E}}\right)^{3}=\left(\frac{T_{P}}{T_{E}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{E}}=\left(\frac{T_{P}}{T_{B}}\right)^{2 / 3}{/tex}
Since {tex}T_P ={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} T_{E}, \frac{T_{P}}{T_{E}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{E}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2 / 3}{/tex} = 0.63

question_text: Let us assume that our galaxy consists of {tex}2.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 }{/tex} stars each of one solar mass. How long will a star at a distance of 50,000 ly from the galactic centre take to complete one revolution? Take the diameter of the Milky Way to be 10 5 ly.
question_html: Let us assume that our galaxy consists of {tex}2.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 }{/tex} stars each of one solar mass. How long will a star at a distance of 50,000 ly from the galactic centre take to complete one revolution? Take the diameter of the Milky Way to be 105 ly.
solution_text: Number of stars in our Galaxy (N) = 2.5 × 10¹¹ Mass of each stars = 2 × 10 30 kg So, mass of the stars of the galaxy(M) = 2.5× 10¹¹× 2× 10 30 = 5 × 10⁴¹ Kg Radius of orbit of a star (r) = 50000 light-years We know, 1 light years = 9.46 × 10 15 m So, r = 50000×9.46×10 15 m = 5 × 9.46 × 10 19 m Centripital force = Gravitational force mv²/r = GMm/r² v² = GM/r (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r/T)² = GM/r [v = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r/T] 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} ²r²/T² = GM/r T² = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} ²r³/GM Put the values of r , G and M T = √{ 4×(3.14)²× (5×9.46×10 19 )³/6.67×10 -11 ×5×10⁴¹} = 111.93 × 10¹⁴ sec = 111.93 × 10¹⁴/(365×24×3600) yr = 3.55 × 10 8 yr
solution_html:
Number of stars in our Galaxy (N) = 2.5 × 10¹¹ 
Mass of each stars = 2 × 1030kg 
So, mass of the stars of the galaxy(M) = 2.5× 10¹¹× 2× 1030
= 5 × 10⁴¹ Kg 
Radius of orbit of a star (r) = 50000 light-years 

We know, 
1 light years = 9.46 × 1015 m
So, r = 50000×9.46×1015 m
= 5 × 9.46 × 1019 m 

Centripital force = Gravitational force 
mv²/r = GMm/r² 
v² = GM/r 
(2{tex}\pi{/tex}r/T)² = GM/r  [v = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r/T]
4{tex}\pi{/tex}²r²/T² = GM/r
T² = 4{tex}\pi{/tex}²r³/GM 
Put the values of r , G and M 
T = √{ 4×(3.14)²× (5×9.46×1019)³/6.67×10-11×5×10⁴¹}
= 111.93 × 10¹⁴ sec 
= 111.93 × 10¹⁴/(365×24×3600) yr
= 3.55 × 108 yr

question_text: Choose the correct alternative: If the zero of potential energy is at infinity, the total energy of an orbiting satellite is negative of its kinetic/potential energy. The energy required to launch an orbiting satellite out of earth's gravitational influence is more/less than the energy required to project a stationary object at the same height (as the satellite) out of earth's influence.
question_html:
Choose the correct alternative:

  1. If the zero of potential energy is at infinity, the total energy of an orbiting satellite is negative of its kinetic/potential energy.
  2. The energy required to launch an orbiting satellite out of earth's gravitational influence is more/less than the energy required to project a stationary object at the same height (as the satellite) out of earth's influence.
solution_text: Kinetic energy Less We know, Potential energy of satellite(P.E) = {tex}\frac {-GMm}r{/tex} Kinetic energy of satellite(K.E) = {tex}\frac {GMm}{2r}{/tex} Total energy = P.E + K.E = {tex}\frac {-GMm}r{/tex} + {tex}\frac {GMm}{2r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {-GMm}{2r}{/tex} = -K.E e.g the total energy of an orbiting satellite is negative of the kinetic energy. As the Earth-satellite system is a bound system, the total energy of the satellite is negative. An orbiting satellite acquires a certain amount of energy that enables it to revolve around the Earth. This energy is provided by its orbit. It requires relatively lesser energy to move out of the influence of the Earth's gravitational field than a stationary object on the Earth's surface that initially contains no energy.
solution_html:
1. Kinetic energy
  2. Less

  1. We know, 
    Potential energy of satellite(P.E) = {tex}\frac {-GMm}r{/tex}
    Kinetic energy of satellite(K.E) = {tex}\frac {GMm}{2r}{/tex}
    Total energy = P.E + K.E 
    = {tex}\frac {-GMm}r{/tex} + {tex}\frac {GMm}{2r}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac {-GMm}{2r}{/tex}= -K.E
    e.g the total energy of an orbiting satellite is negative of the kinetic energy.
    As the Earth-satellite system is a bound system, the total energy of the satellite is negative.

  2. An orbiting satellite acquires a certain amount of energy that enables it to revolve around the Earth. This energy is provided by its orbit. It requires relatively lesser energy to move out of the influence of the Earth's gravitational field than a stationary object on the Earth's surface that initially contains no energy.

question_text: Does the escape speed of a body from the earth depend on mass of the body the location from where it is projected the direction of projection the height of the location from where the body is launched?

Does the escape speed of a body from the earth depend on

  1. mass of the body
  2. the location from where it is projected
  3. the direction of projection
  4. the height of the location from where the body is launched?
solution_text: No, escape velocity does not depend upon the mass of the body. Yes, escape velocity depends on the location from where the body is projected because with location, g changes which results in the change of {tex}v_e (={/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2 g R} ){/tex} . No, escape velocity does not depend upon the direction of projection. Yes, escape velocity depends on the height of location from where the body is projected as value of g depends on the height and change in g results in the change of {tex}v_e{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. No, escape velocity does not depend upon the mass of the body.
  2. Yes, escape velocity depends on the location from where the body is projected because with location, g changes which results in the change of {tex}v_e (={/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2 g R} ){/tex}.
  3. No, escape velocity does not depend upon the direction of projection.
  4. Yes, escape velocity depends on the height of location from where the body is projected as value of g depends on the height and change in g results in the change of {tex}v_e{/tex}.

question_text: A comet orbits the sun in highly elliptical orbit. Does the comet has a constant linear speed, angular speed, angular momentum, kinetic energy, potential energy and total energy throughout its orbit? Neglect any mass loss of the comet when it comes very close to the sun.
question_html:
A comet orbits the sun in highly elliptical orbit. Does the comet has a constant

  1. linear speed,
  2. angular speed,
  3. angular momentum,
  4. kinetic energy,
  5. potential energy and
  6. total energy throughout its orbit?

Neglect any mass loss of the comet when it comes very close to the sun.
solution_text: A comet while going on elliptical orbit around the Sun has constant angular momentum due to no external torque and total energy due to Law of conservation of energy at all locations but other quantities vary with locations. The speed of the comet does not remain constant because according to the law of conservation of angular momentum, L = mvr = constant, therefore the comet moves faster when it is close to the sun and moves slower when it is farther away from the sun. The angular speed of the comet does not remain constant because the linear speed varies, the angular speed also varies. As no external torque is acting on the comet, therefore, according to law of conservation of angular momentum, the angular momentum of the comet remains constant. The kinetic energy of the comet = {tex}\frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex} Its KE does not remain constant because the linear speed of the comet changes, its kinetic energy also changes. The potential energy of the comet changes as its kinetic energy changes. Only angular momentum and total energy of a comet remain constant throughout its orbit.
solution_html:
1. A comet while going on elliptical orbit around the Sun has constant angular momentum due to no external torque and total energy due to Law of conservation of energy at all locations but other quantities vary with locations. The speed of the comet does not remain constant because according to the law of conservation of angular momentum, L = mvr = constant, therefore the comet moves faster when it is close to the sun and moves slower when it is farther away from the sun. 
  2. The angular speed of the comet does not remain constant because the linear speed varies, the angular speed also varies. 
  3. As no external torque is acting on the comet, therefore, according to law of conservation of angular momentum, the angular momentum of the comet remains constant.
  4. The kinetic energy of the comet = {tex}\frac{1}{2}mv^2{/tex}
    Its KE does not remain constant because the linear speed of the comet changes, its kinetic energy also changes.
  5. The potential energy of the comet changes as its kinetic energy changes.
  6. Only angular momentum and total energy of a comet remain constant throughout its orbit.

question_text: Which of the following symptoms is likely to affect an astronaut in space swollen feet swollen face headache Orientational problem
question_html:
Which of the following symptoms is likely to affect an astronaut in space

  1. swollen feet
  2. swollen face
  3. headache
  4. Orientational problem
solution_text: The astronaut in space is in weightlessness state. The legs carry the weight of the body in the normal position due to gravity pull. Therefore, swollen feet may not affect. As eyes, ears, nose, mouth etc., are all embedded in the face, the swollen face may affect to great extent the seeing/hearing/smelling/eating capabilities of the astronaut in space. because in the conditions of weightlessness, the face of the astronaut is expected to get more supply. Due to it, the astronaut may develop swollen face. Headache is due to mental strain. It will persist whether a person is an astronaut in space or he is on earth. It means headache will have the same effect on the astronaut in space as on a person on earth. Space also has orientation. We also have the frames of reference in space. Hence, the orientational problem will affect astronaut in space.
solution_html:
1. The astronaut in space is in weightlessness state. The legs carry the weight of the body in the normal position due to gravity pull. Therefore, swollen feet may not affect.
  2. As eyes, ears, nose, mouth etc., are all embedded in the face, the swollen face may affect to great extent the seeing/hearing/smelling/eating capabilities of the astronaut in space. because in the conditions of weightlessness, the face of the astronaut is expected to get more supply. Due to it, the astronaut may develop swollen face.
  3. Headache is due to mental strain. It will persist whether a person is an astronaut in space or he is on earth. It means headache will have the same effect on the astronaut in space as on a person on earth.
  4. Space also has orientation. We also have the frames of reference in space. Hence, the orientational problem will affect astronaut in space.

question_text: A rocket is fired from the earth towards the sun. At what distance from the earth's centre is the gravitational force on the rocket zero? Mass of the sun = {tex} 2 \times 10 ^ { 30 }{/tex} kg, mass of the earth = {tex} 6 \times 10 ^ { 24 }{/tex} kg. Neglect the effect of other planets etc. (orbital radius = {tex} 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 }{/tex} m).
question_html: A rocket is fired from the earth towards the sun. At what distance from the earth's centre is the gravitational force on the rocket zero? Mass of the sun = {tex} 2 \times 10 ^ { 30 }{/tex} kg, mass of the earth = {tex} 6 \times 10 ^ { 24 }{/tex} kg. Neglect the effect of other planets etc. (orbital radius = {tex} 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 }{/tex} m).
solution_text: There is a point called the La-Grange L1 point roughly in a million miles from earth towards the sun. It could be considered that the net gravitational force at this point is zero. Practically it can never be zero. However, there are places where there is so little gravity that it becomes an insignificant factor, and we consider gravity at that point to be zero. Given parameters are: Mass of the Sun, {tex}M _ { s } = 2 \times 10 ^ { 30 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex} Mass of the Earth, {tex}M _ { \theta } = 6 \times 10 ^ { 24 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex} Orbital radius, {tex}r = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Mass of the rocket = m Let's suppose that x be the distance from the centre of the Earth where the gravitational force acting on satellite P becomes zero. From Newton's law of gravitation, we can equate gravitational forces acting on satellite P under the influence of the Sun and the Earth as: {tex}\frac { G m M _ { s } } { ( r - x ) ^ { 2 } }= \frac { GmM _ { \theta } } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\left( \frac { r - x } { x } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { M _ { s } } { M _ { \theta } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { r - x } { x } = \left( \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { 30 } } { 6\times 10 ^ { 24 } } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } = 577.35{/tex} 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 - x = 577.35 x 577.35 x = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 {tex}x = \frac { 1.5 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 11 } } { 578.35 }{/tex} = 2.59 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m. Now we can say that this is the distance at which the gravitational force on the rocket from earth's centre is zero.
solution_html:
There is a point called the La-Grange L1 point roughly in a million miles from earth towards the sun. It could be considered that the net gravitational force at this point is zero. Practically it can never be zero. However, there are places where there is so little gravity that it becomes an insignificant factor, and we consider gravity at that point to be zero.
Given parameters are:
Mass of the Sun, {tex}M _ { s } = 2 \times 10 ^ { 30 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex}
Mass of the Earth, {tex}M _ { \theta } = 6 \times 10 ^ { 24 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex}
Orbital radius, {tex}r = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Mass of the rocket = m

Let's suppose that x be the distance from the centre of the Earth where the gravitational force acting on satellite P becomes zero.
From Newton's law of gravitation, we can equate gravitational forces acting on satellite P under the influence of the Sun and the Earth as:
{tex}\frac { G m M _ { s } } { ( r - x ) ^ { 2 } }= \frac { GmM _ { \theta } } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\left( \frac { r - x } { x } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { M _ { s } } { M _ { \theta } }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { r - x } { x } = \left( \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { 30 } } { 6\times 10 ^ { 24 } } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } = 577.35{/tex}
1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 - x = 577.35 x
577.35 x = 1.5  {tex}\times{/tex} 1011
{tex}x = \frac { 1.5 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 11 } } { 578.35 }{/tex} = 2.59  {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m.
Now we can say that this is the distance at which the gravitational force on the rocket from earth's centre is zero.

question_text: How will you weigh the sun, that is estimate its mass? The mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun is {tex}1.5 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} km.
question_html: How will you weigh the sun, that is estimate its mass? The mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun is {tex}1.5 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} km.
solution_text: The orbital period is the time a given astronomical object takes to complete one orbit around another object, and applies in astronomy usually to planets or asteroids orbiting the Sun, moons orbiting planets, exoplanets orbiting other stars, or binary stars. Orbital radius of the Earth around the Sun, {tex}r = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Time taken by the Earth to complete one revolution around the Sun, T = 1 year = 365.25 days = 365.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60s Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 Mm 2 kg -2 {tex}M = \frac { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 3 } } { G t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 \ \times \ ( 3.14 ) ^ { 2 } \times \left( 1.5 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 11 } \right) ^ { 3 } } { 6.67 \times 10 ^ { - 11 } \times ( 365.25 \ \times \ 24 \ \times \ 60 \ \times \ 60 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 133.24 \ \times \ 10 } { 6.64 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 4 } } = 2.0 \times 10 ^ { 30 } k g{/tex} Hence, the mass of the Sun is {tex}2 \times 10 ^ { 30 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex}
solution_html:
The orbital period is the time a given astronomical object takes to complete one orbit around another object, and applies in astronomy usually to planets or asteroids orbiting the Sun, moons orbiting planets, exoplanets orbiting other stars, or binary stars.
Orbital radius of the Earth around the Sun, {tex}r = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Time taken by the Earth to complete one revolution around the Sun,
T = 1 year = 365.25 days
= 365.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60s
Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 Mm2kg-2
{tex}M = \frac { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } r ^ { 3 } } { G t ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 \ \times \ ( 3.14 ) ^ { 2 } \times \left( 1.5 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 11 } \right) ^ { 3 } } { 6.67 \times 10 ^ { - 11 } \times ( 365.25 \ \times \ 24 \ \times \ 60 \ \times \ 60 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 133.24 \ \times \ 10 } { 6.64 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 4 } } = 2.0 \times 10 ^ { 30 } k g{/tex}
Hence, the mass of the Sun is {tex}2 \times 10 ^ { 30 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex}

question_text: A Saturn year is 29.5 times the earth year. How far is the Saturn from the sun if the earth is {tex}1.50 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} km away from the sun?
question_html: A Saturn year is 29.5 times the earth year. How far is the Saturn from the sun if the earth is {tex}1.50 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{/tex} km away from the sun?
solution_text: Distance of the Earth from the Sun, {tex}\mathrm { re } = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \mathrm { km } = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { \mathrm { 11 } } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Time period of Saturn, T s = 29.5T e Distance of Saturn from the Sun = r s From Kepler's 3rd law of planetary motion {tex}T^2 = r^3{/tex} For the case of Saturn and Sun {tex}\frac { T _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { T _ { e } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { s } ^ { 3 } } { r _ { e } ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}r _ { s } = r _ { e } \left( \frac { T _ { s } } { T _ { e } } \right) ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }{/tex} ..................(1) put the value of r e ,T e and T s in equation (1) {tex}r_s=1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \left( \frac { 29.5 T _ { e } } { T _ { e } } \right) ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}r_s=1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } ( 29.5 ) ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \times 9.55{/tex} {tex}r_s= 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \times 9.55{/tex} {tex}r_s= 14.32 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Hence, the distance between Saturn and the Sun is {tex}1.43 \times 10 ^ { 12 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Distance of the Earth from the Sun, {tex}\mathrm { re } = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \mathrm { km } = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { \mathrm { 11 } } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Time period of Saturn, Ts = 29.5Te
Distance of Saturn from the Sun = rs
From Kepler's 3rd law of planetary motion {tex}T^2 = r^3{/tex}
For the case of Saturn and Sun {tex}\frac { T _ { s } ^ { 2 } } { T _ { e } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { r _ { s } ^ { 3 } } { r _ { e } ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex}r _ { s } = r _ { e } \left( \frac { T _ { s } } { T _ { e } } \right) ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }{/tex}..................(1)

put the value of re,Te and Ts in equation (1)
{tex}r_s=1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \left( \frac { 29.5 T _ { e } } { T _ { e } } \right) ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex}r_s=1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } ( 29.5 ) ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } = 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \times 9.55{/tex}

{tex}r_s= 1.5 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \times 9.55{/tex}
{tex}r_s= 14.32 \times 10 ^ { 11 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Hence, the distance between Saturn and the Sun is {tex}1.43 \times 10 ^ { 12 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}.

question_text: A body weighs 63 N on the surface of the earth. What is the gravitational force on it due to the earth at a height equal to half the radius of the earth?
question_html: A body weighs 63 N on the surface of the earth. What is the gravitational force on it due to the earth at a height equal to half the radius of the earth?
solution_text: Suppose g h be the acceleration due to gravity at a height equal to half the radius of the earth h = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} As we know, {tex}\frac{g_{h}}{g}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{R}{R+h}\right)^{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{g_{h}}{g}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{R}{R+\frac{R}{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex} W = the weight of the body on the surface of the earth W h = the weight of the body at height h. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} , {tex}\frac{W_{h}}{W}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{m g_{h}}{m g}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{g_{h}}{g}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} W h= {tex}\frac{4}{9}W= \frac{4}{9} \times 63 N = 28 N{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose gh be the acceleration due to gravity at a height equal to half the radius of the earth h = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}
As we know,
 {tex}\frac{g_{h}}{g}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{R}{R+h}\right)^{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{g_{h}}{g}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{R}{R+\frac{R}{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
W = the weight of the body on the surface of the earth
Wh = the weight of the body at height h.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}, {tex}\frac{W_{h}}{W}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{m g_{h}}{m g}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{g_{h}}{g}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} Wh= {tex}\frac{4}{9}W=   \frac{4}{9} \times 63 N = 28 N{/tex}

question_text: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, how much would body weigh halfway down to the centre of the earth if it weighted 250 N on the surface?
question_html: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, how much would body weigh halfway down to the centre of the earth if it weighted 250 N on the surface?
solution_text: Weight of the body at the earth’s surface w = mg = 250 N ...(i) Acceleration due to gravity at depth d from the earth’s surface {tex}g' = g{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right){/tex} here, {tex}d ={/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}g' = g{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{R / 2}{R}\right){/tex} = g {tex}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}g' ={/tex} {tex}\frac{g}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The weight of the body at depth d {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}w' = mg' ={/tex} {tex}\frac{m g}{2}{/tex} Using Eq. (i) we get {tex}w' ={/tex} {tex}\frac{250}{2}{/tex} = 125 N {tex}\therefore{/tex} Weight of the body will be 125 N.
solution_html:
Weight of the body at the earth’s surface
w = mg = 250 N ...(i)
Acceleration due to gravity at depth d from the earth’s surface
{tex}g' = g{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right){/tex}
here, {tex}d ={/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}g' = g{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{R / 2}{R}\right){/tex} = g {tex}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}g' ={/tex} {tex}\frac{g}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The weight of the body at depth d
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}w' = mg' ={/tex} {tex}\frac{m g}{2}{/tex}
Using Eq. (i) we get
{tex}w' ={/tex} {tex}\frac{250}{2}{/tex} = 125 N
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Weight of the body will be 125 N.

question_text: A rocket is fired vertically with a speed of 5 km s -1 from the earth's surface. How far from the earth does the rocket go before returning to the earth? Mass of the earth = {tex}6.0 \times 10 ^ { 24 }{/tex} kg; mean radius of the earth = {tex}6.4 \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} m; {tex}G = 6.67 \times 10 ^ { - 11 } \mathbf { N } m ^ { 2 } k g ^ { - 2 }{/tex} .
question_html: A rocket is fired vertically with a speed of 5 km s-1 from the earth's surface. How far from the earth does the rocket go before returning to the earth? solution_text: Data Given: Velocity of the rocket, v = 5 km/s = {tex}5 \times 10 ^ { 3 }{/tex} m/s Mass of the Earth, {tex}M _ { \varepsilon } = 6.0 \times 10 ^ { 24 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex} Radius of the Earth, {tex}R _ { \varepsilon } = 6.4 \times 10 ^ { 6 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Height reached by rocket of mass m is h At the surface of the Earth, Total energy of the rocket = Kinetic energy + Potential energy {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { - G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } } \right){/tex} At highest point h, v = 0 And, Potential energy {tex}- \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h }{/tex} Total energy of the rocket {tex}= 0 + \left( - \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h } \right) = - \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h }{/tex} From the law of conservation of energy, we have Total energy of the rocket at the Earth's surface = Total energy at height h {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } + \left( - \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } v ^ { 2 } = G M _ { \varepsilon } \left( \frac { 1 } { R _ { \varepsilon } } - \frac { 1 } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h } \right){/tex} {tex}G M _ { \varepsilon } \left( \frac { R _ { \varepsilon } \ + \ h \ - \ R _ { \varepsilon } } { R _ { \varepsilon } \left( R _ { \varepsilon } \ + \ h \right) } \right){/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } v ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } h } { R _ { \varepsilon } \left( R _ { \varepsilon } \ + \ h \right) } \times \frac { R _ { \varepsilon } } { R _ { \varepsilon } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } v ^ { 2 } = \frac { g R_ {\varepsilon} h } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h }{/tex} Where {tex}g = \frac { G M } { R _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } = 9.8 \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Acceleration due to gravity on the Earth’s surface) {tex}\therefore v ^ { 2 } \left( R _ { \varepsilon } + h \right) = 2 g R _ { \varepsilon } h{/tex} {tex}v ^ { 2 } R _ { \varepsilon } = h \left( 2 g R _ { \varepsilon } - v ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}h = \frac { R _ { \varepsilon } v ^ { 2 } } { 2 g R _ { \varepsilon } \ -\ v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 6.4 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } \ \times \ \left( 5 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \ \times \ 9.8 \ \times \ 6.4 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } - \left( 5 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} h = {tex}\frac { 6.4 \ \times \ 25 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 12 } } { 100.44 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6} }{/tex} = = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m Height achieved by the rocket with respect to the centre of the Earth {tex}= R _ { \varepsilon } + h{/tex} = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 + 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m = 8.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m
solution_html:
Data Given:
Velocity of the rocket, v = 5 km/s = {tex}5 \times 10 ^ { 3 }{/tex} m/s
Mass of the Earth, {tex}M _ { \varepsilon } = 6.0 \times 10 ^ { 24 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex}
Radius of the Earth,{tex}R _ { \varepsilon } = 6.4 \times 10 ^ { 6 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Height reached by rocket of mass m is h
At the surface of the Earth,
Total energy of the rocket = Kinetic energy + Potential energy
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { - G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } } \right){/tex}
At highest point h, v = 0
And, Potential energy {tex}- \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h }{/tex}
Total energy of the rocket {tex}= 0 + \left( - \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h } \right) = - \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h }{/tex}
From the law of conservation of energy, we have
Total energy of the rocket at the Earth's surface = Total energy at height h
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } + \left( - \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h } \right){/tex} {tex}= - \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } m } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } v ^ { 2 } = G M _ { \varepsilon } \left( \frac { 1 } { R _ { \varepsilon } } - \frac { 1 } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h } \right){/tex}
{tex}G M _ { \varepsilon } \left( \frac { R _ { \varepsilon } \ + \ h \ - \ R _ { \varepsilon } } { R _ { \varepsilon } \left( R _ { \varepsilon } \ + \ h \right) } \right){/tex}
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } v ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { G M _ { \varepsilon } h } { R _ { \varepsilon } \left( R _ { \varepsilon } \ + \ h \right) } \times \frac { R _ { \varepsilon } } { R _ { \varepsilon } }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } v ^ { 2 } = \frac { g R_ {\varepsilon} h } { R _ { \varepsilon } + h }{/tex}
Where {tex}g = \frac { G M } { R _ { \varepsilon } ^ { 2 } } = 9.8 \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Acceleration due to gravity on the Earth’s surface)
{tex}\therefore v ^ { 2 } \left( R _ { \varepsilon } + h \right) = 2 g R _ { \varepsilon } h{/tex}
{tex}v ^ { 2 } R _ { \varepsilon } = h \left( 2 g R _ { \varepsilon } - v ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}h = \frac { R _ { \varepsilon } v ^ { 2 } } { 2 g R _ { \varepsilon } \ -\ v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 6.4 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } \ \times \ \left( 5 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \ \times \ 9.8 \ \times \ 6.4 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } - \left( 5 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
h = {tex}\frac { 6.4 \ \times \ 25 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 12 } } { 100.44 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6} }{/tex} = = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m
Height achieved by the rocket with respect to the centre of the Earth
{tex}= R _ { \varepsilon } + h{/tex}
= 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 + 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m
= 8.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m

question_html: The escape speed of a projectile on the earth’s surface is 11.2 km/s. A body is projected out with thrice this speed. What is the speed of the body far away from the earth? Ignore the presence of the sun and other planets.
solution_text: v e = escape velocity v = velocity of the body outside the gravitational field of the earth. According to the law of conservation of energy, Initial KE of the body = energy spent by the body in crossing the earth’s gravitational field + kinetic energy left with the body once outside the earth’s gravitational field, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}m(3v_e)^2 ={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}m{/tex} {tex}v_{e}^{2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}mv^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\frac{9}{2}{/tex} m {tex}v_{e}^{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}m{/tex} {tex}v_{e}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}mv^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}v^2 = 8{/tex} {tex}v_{c}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}v ={/tex} {tex}\sqrt{8 v_{e}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \sqrt{2} v_{e}{/tex} As {tex}v_e = 11.2 km/s{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}v ={/tex} {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}11.2 km/s{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} v = 31.7 km/s

question_text: A satellite orbits the earth at a height of 400 km above the surface. How much energy must be expended to rocket the satellite out of the earth's gravitational influence? Mass of the satellite = 200 kg; mass of the earth = {tex}6.0 \times 10 ^ { 24 }{/tex} kg; radius of the earth = {tex}6.4 \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} m; G = {tex}6.67 \times 10 ^ { - 11 } \mathrm { N } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { kg } ^ { - 2 }{/tex} .
question_html: A satellite orbits the earth at a height of 400 km above the surface. How much energy must be expended to rocket the satellite out of the earth's gravitational influence? Mass of the satellite = 200 kg; mass of the earth = {tex}6.0 \times 10 ^ { 24 }{/tex} kg; radius of the earth = {tex}6.4 \times 10 ^ { 6 }{/tex} m; G = {tex}6.67 \times 10 ^ { - 11 } \mathrm { N } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { kg } ^ { - 2 }{/tex}.
solution_text: Me = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 kg Mass of the satellite, m = 200 kg R e = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m G = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 Nm 2 kg -2 Height of the satellite, h = 400 km = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 m = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m Total energy of the satellite at height h =Kinectic energy + Potential energy Total energy of the satellite at height h {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { - G M _ { e } m } { R _ { e } + h } \right){/tex} Orbital velocity of the satellite, {tex}v = \sqrt { \frac { G M _ { e } } { R _ { x } + h } }{/tex} Total energy= {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } m \left( \frac { G M _ { e } } { R _ { e } + h } \right) - \frac { G M _ { e } m } { R _ { e } + h } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { G M _ { e } m } { R _ { e } + h } \right){/tex} Energy required to send the satellite out of its orbit = -(Bound energy) {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { G M _ { e } m } { \left( R _ { e } + h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 6.67 \ \times \ 10 ^ { - 11 } \ \times\ 6.0 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 24 } \ \times \ 200 } { \left( 6.4 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } \ + \ 0.4 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6} \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 6.67 \ \times \ 10 ^ { - 11 } \ \times \ 12 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 26 } } { \left( 6.8 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 6.67 \ \times \ 12 \ \times \ 10^9 } { 6.8 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } } = 5.9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } J{/tex}
solution_html:
Me = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 kg
Mass of the satellite, m = 200 kg
Re = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m 
G = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 Nm2 kg-2
Height of the satellite, h = 400 km = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 m = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m
Total energy of the satellite at height h =Kinectic energy + Potential energy
Total energy of the satellite at height h {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { - G M _ { e } m } { R _ { e } + h } \right){/tex}
Orbital velocity of the satellite, {tex}v = \sqrt { \frac { G M _ { e } } { R _ { x } + h } }{/tex}
Total energy={tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } m \left( \frac { G M _ { e } } { R _ { e } + h } \right) - \frac { G M _ { e } m } { R _ { e } + h } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { G M _ { e } m } { R _ { e } + h } \right){/tex}
Energy required to send the satellite out of its orbit = -(Bound energy)
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { G M _ { e } m } { \left( R _ { e } + h \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 6.67 \ \times \ 10 ^ { - 11 } \ \times\ 6.0 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 24 } \ \times \ 200 } { \left( 6.4 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } \ + \ 0.4 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6} \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 6.67 \ \times \ 10 ^ { - 11 } \ \times \ 12 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 26 } } { \left( 6.8 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 6.67 \ \times \ 12 \ \times \ 10^9 } { 6.8 \ \times \ 10 ^ { 6 } } = 5.9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } J{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Two heavy spheres each of mass 100 kg and radius 0.10 m are placed 1.0 m apart on a horizontal table. What is the gravitational force and potential at the midpoint of the line joining the centres of the spheres? Is an object placed at that point in equilibrium? If so, is the equilibrium stable or unstable?
question_html: Two heavy spheres each of mass 100 kg and radius 0.10 m are placed 1.0 m apart on a horizontal table. What is the gravitational force and potential at the midpoint of the line joining the centres of the spheres? Is an object placed at that point in equilibrium? If so, is the equilibrium stable or unstable?
solution_text: Gravitational potential at the midpoint of the line joining the centres of the two spheres is {tex}V = \frac{{GM}}{{\frac{r}{2}}} - \frac{{GM}}{{\frac{r}{2}}} = \frac{{4GM}}{r}{/tex} {tex}V = \frac{{4 \times 6.67 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times 100}}{{0.1}}{/tex} = -2.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 J/kg As the effective force on the body placed at mid-point is zero, so the body is in equilibrium. If the body is displaced a little towards either mass body from its equilibrium position, it will not return back to its initial position of equilibrium. Hence, the body is in an unstable equilibrium.
solution_html:
Gravitational potential at the midpoint of the line joining the centres of the two spheres is
{tex}V = \frac{{GM}}{{\frac{r}{2}}} - \frac{{GM}}{{\frac{r}{2}}} = \frac{{4GM}}{r}{/tex}
{tex}V = \frac{{4 \times 6.67 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times 100}}{{0.1}}{/tex}
= -2.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 J/kg
As the effective force on the body placed at mid-point is zero, so the body is in equilibrium. If the body is displaced a little towards either mass body from its equilibrium position, it will not return back to its initial position of equilibrium. Hence, the body is in an unstable equilibrium.
